passages.explanation: After the resurrection appearances in Jerusalem, Jesus reveals himself again at the Sea of Tiberias (Galilee). Seven disciples are together: Peter, Thomas, Nathanael, the sons of Zebedee, and two others. Peter announces he's going fishing and the others join him. They fish all night and catch nothing. They've gone back to their old occupation — the resurrection has happened but they don't yet know what comes next.

At dawn, Jesus stands on the shore but the disciples don't recognise him. He calls out and asks if they've caught anything. They say no. He tells them to cast the net on the right side of the boat. They do, and the catch is so large they can't haul it in. The beloved disciple recognises what's happening and tells Peter 'It's the Lord.' Peter immediately wraps his outer garment around himself and jumps into the water to get to Jesus. The others follow in the boat, dragging the net full of fish.

When they reach shore, they find Jesus has already prepared a charcoal fire with fish and bread cooking on it. He tells them to bring some of the fish they just caught. Peter hauls the net ashore — 153 large fish, and the net isn't torn. Jesus invites them to eat breakfast. None of them dares ask who he is — they know it's the Lord. Jesus serves them bread and fish. John notes this is the third time Jesus appeared to the disciples after the resurrection.

After breakfast, Jesus turns to Peter and asks three times whether Peter loves him. Each time Peter says yes, and each time Jesus gives him a charge: feed my lambs, tend my sheep, feed my sheep. Peter is grieved by the third question. The three questions mirror Peter's three denials in chapter 18. Jesus is restoring Peter to his role — not by ignoring the failure, but by giving Peter three opportunities to affirm his loyalty corresponding to the three times he denied it. Each affirmation comes with a responsibility: care for Jesus' followers.

Jesus tells Peter that when he was young he dressed himself and went where he wanted, but when he's old he'll stretch out his hands and be carried where he doesn't want to go. John explains that Jesus was indicating the kind of death Peter would face — tradition holds that Peter was eventually crucified. After this prediction, Jesus says to Peter the same two words he said at their very first meeting: 'Follow me.'

Peter turns and sees the beloved disciple following them. He asks Jesus what will happen to that disciple. Jesus' response is pointed: if I want him to remain alive until I return, what is that to you? You follow me. A rumour spread among the early believers that this disciple wouldn't die, but John corrects it — Jesus didn't say he wouldn't die, only posed a hypothetical to redirect Peter's attention. The message is clear: each disciple has their own path, and Peter's job is to follow his, not worry about someone else's.

The author identifies the beloved disciple as the one who testifies to these things and wrote them down. The community behind the gospel vouches for his testimony: 'We know that his witness is true.' The gospel closes by saying Jesus did many other things that aren't recorded — so many that the author suggests the world itself couldn't contain all the books that would need to be written.
